```{r tornado_eval_fn}
  p_bequest <- mean(terminal_wealth >= p$bequest_target)
  list(p_ruin = p_ruin, p_bequest = p_bequest)
}

# Baseline (should match result$best_ruin exactly)
baseline <- eval_with_overrides(result$best_beta)
cat(sprintf("Baseline P(ruin)    = %.3f%%  (optimizer reported: %.3f%%)\n",
            baseline$p_ruin * 100, result$best_ruin * 100))
cat(sprintf("Baseline P(bequest) = %.3f%%\n", baseline$p_bequest * 100))
```

## Tornado 1: Symmetric +/-10% Elasticity

Every parameter perturbed by the same relative amount. Makes bars directly comparable. Integer-valued parameters (SS start age, RMD start age) use +/-2 years, the realistic decision/legislative window.

```{r tornado_run_sym}
# Helper to format numeric parameter values with an appropriate unit
fmt_val <- function(x, kind){
  switch(kind,
    dollar = sprintf("$%s", format(round(x), big.mark = ",")),
    pct    = sprintf("%.1f%%", x * 100),
    year   = sprintf("age %d", as.integer(x)),
    yrs    = sprintf("%.1f yrs", x),
    as.character(x)
  )
}

params_sym <- list(
  list(name = "Annual spending",       key = "annual_spending",     kind = "dollar", base = annual_spending,     lo = 0.90 * annual_spending,     hi = 1.10 * annual_spending),
  list(name = "Current savings",       key = "current_savings",     kind = "dollar", base = current_savings,     lo = 0.90 * current_savings,     hi = 1.10 * current_savings),
  list(name = "Current income",        key = "current_income",      kind = "dollar", base = current_income,      lo = 0.90 * current_income,      hi = 1.10 * current_income),
  list(name = "Savings rate",          key = "savings_rate",        kind = "pct",    base = savings_rate,        lo = 0.90 * savings_rate,        hi = 1.10 * savings_rate),
  list(name = "Real income growth",    key = "real_income_growth",  kind = "pct",    base = real_income_growth,  lo = 0.90 * real_income_growth,  hi = 1.10 * real_income_growth),
  list(name = "SS annual benefit",     key = "ss_benefit",          kind = "dollar", base = ss_benefit,          lo = 0.90 * ss_benefit,          hi = 1.10 * ss_benefit),
  list(name = "SS start age",          key = "ss_start_age",        kind = "year",   base = ss_start_age,        lo = ss_start_age - 2,           hi = ss_start_age + 2),
  list(name = "Retirement tax rate",   key = "retirement_tax_rate", kind = "pct",    base = retirement_tax_rate, lo = 0.90 * retirement_tax_rate, hi = 1.10 * retirement_tax_rate)
)

run_tornado <- function(plist){
  do.call(rbind, lapply(plist, function(p){
    lo_res <- eval_with_overrides(result$best_beta, setNames(list(p$lo), p$key))
    hi_res <- eval_with_overrides(result$best_beta, setNames(list(p$hi), p$key))
    data.frame(
      parameter  = p$name,
      kind       = p$kind,
      base_val   = p$base,
      lo_val     = p$lo,
      hi_val     = p$hi,
      ruin_lo    = lo_res$p_ruin,
      ruin_hi    = hi_res$p_ruin,
      bequest_lo = lo_res$p_bequest,
      bequest_hi = hi_res$p_bequest,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  })) %>%
    mutate(
      ruin_swing = abs(ruin_hi - ruin_lo),
      lo_delta   = (ruin_lo - baseline$p_ruin) * 100,
      hi_delta   = (ruin_hi - baseline$p_ruin) * 100,
      lo_abs     = ruin_lo * 100,
      hi_abs     = ruin_hi * 100,
      value_ctx  = mapply(function(b, l, h, k) sprintf("base %s  |  %s / %s",
                                                       fmt_val(b, k), fmt_val(l, k), fmt_val(h, k)),
                          base_val, lo_val, hi_val, kind)
    ) %>%
    arrange(ruin_swing) %>%
    mutate(parameter = factor(parameter, levels = parameter))
}

tornado_sym <- run_tornado(params_sym)

# Print explicit table: absolute and delta P(ruin) for each perturbation
cat("\n=== Tornado 1: Symmetric +/-10% perturbations ===\n")
cat(sprintf("Baseline P(ruin) = %.3f%%\n\n", baseline$p_ruin * 100))
print_df <- tornado_sym %>%
  arrange(desc(ruin_swing)) %>%
  transmute(
    Parameter          = as.character(parameter),
    `Base | Lo / Hi`   = value_ctx,
    `P(ruin) lo`       = sprintf("%.3f%% (%+.3fpp)", lo_abs, lo_delta),
    `P(ruin) hi`       = sprintf("%.3f%% (%+.3fpp)", hi_abs, hi_delta),
    `Swing (pp)`       = sprintf("%.3f", ruin_swing * 100)
  )
print(print_df, row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r tornado_plot_sym, fig.width=11, fig.height=6.5}
# Enriched tornado plot: show baseline as solid line, absolute P(ruin) at each end
ggplot(tornado_sym, aes(y = parameter)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = lo_abs, xend = hi_abs, yend = parameter),
               linewidth = 8, color = "steelblue", alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = baseline$p_ruin * 100, linetype = "solid",
             color = "grey20", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(x = lo_abs,
                label = sprintf("%.2f%%\n(%+.2fpp)", lo_abs, lo_delta)),
            hjust = 1.1, size = 2.8, color = "grey15", lineheight = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(x = hi_abs,
                label = sprintf("%.2f%%\n(%+.2fpp)", hi_abs, hi_delta)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 2.8, color = "grey15", lineheight = 0.9) +
  annotate("text", x = baseline$p_ruin * 100,
           y = length(levels(tornado_sym$parameter)) + 0.3,
           label = sprintf("baseline = %.2f%%", baseline$p_ruin * 100),
           size = 3.3, color = "grey20", hjust = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Tornado 1: \u0394 P(ruin) under symmetric +/-10% perturbations",
       subtitle = sprintf("Each bar spans lo (-10%%) to hi (+10%%). Vertical line = baseline P(ruin) = %.2f%%.",
                          baseline$p_ruin * 100),
       x = "P(ruin) (%)  (absolute on axis, delta in parentheses)", y = "",
       caption = "Parameters sorted by swing magnitude. Wider = more sensitive.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        plot.margin = margin(12, 40, 12, 40))
```

## Tornado 2: Calibrated Realistic Uncertainty Bands

Not every parameter is equally uncertain. The bands below reflect order-of-magnitude "how well do I actually know this" estimates. This reveals that the P(ruin) sensitivity rankings change meaningfully once you account for realistic uncertainty: a parameter that is elastic but well-known moves down the chart; a parameter that is inelastic but highly uncertain can move up.

Uncertainty band rationale:

- **Current savings** (+/-1%): known to the dollar from account statements; the only uncertainty is timing and valuation noise.
- **Current income** (+/-5%): the paystub is exact for this year, but income can move with promotions/job changes.
- **Savings rate** (+/-20%): under personal control but subject to budget shocks (home purchase, kids, medical), so a 2-3pp absolute band is realistic.
- **Annual spending** (+/-20%): retirees often underestimate their actual spending; the Society of Actuaries has published retrospective analyses showing realized spending diverges from budgeted by 15-25%.
- **Real income growth** (+/-50%): 35-year projection errors compound; could plausibly be 0.75% or 2.25% rather than the 1.5% baseline.
- **SS annual benefit** (-25% / +5%): asymmetric because the SSA trustee reports indicate legislative-gap scenarios could cut benefits 20-25% in the 2030s absent action; upside is capped by the benefit formula.
- **SS start age** (+/-5 years): spans the full claim-at-62 to delay-to-70 decision.
- **Retirement tax rate** (+/-30%): legislative risk on federal brackets, potential state moves, filing-status changes post-spouse's death.

```{r tornado_run_calibrated}
params_calib <- list(
  list(name = "Annual spending (+/-20%)",        key = "annual_spending",     kind = "dollar", base = annual_spending,     lo = 0.80 * annual_spending,     hi = 1.20 * annual_spending),
  list(name = "Current savings (+/-1%)",         key = "current_savings",     kind = "dollar", base = current_savings,     lo = 0.99 * current_savings,     hi = 1.01 * current_savings),
  list(name = "Current income (+/-5%)",          key = "current_income",      kind = "dollar", base = current_income,      lo = 0.95 * current_income,      hi = 1.05 * current_income),
  list(name = "Savings rate (+/-20%)",           key = "savings_rate",        kind = "pct",    base = savings_rate,        lo = 0.80 * savings_rate,        hi = 1.20 * savings_rate),
  list(name = "Real income growth (+/-50%)",     key = "real_income_growth",  kind = "pct",    base = real_income_growth,  lo = 0.50 * real_income_growth,  hi = 1.50 * real_income_growth),
  list(name = "SS benefit (-25% / +5%)",         key = "ss_benefit",          kind = "dollar", base = ss_benefit,          lo = 0.75 * ss_benefit,          hi = 1.05 * ss_benefit),
  list(name = "SS start age (+/-5y)",            key = "ss_start_age",        kind = "year",   base = ss_start_age,        lo = ss_start_age - 5,           hi = ss_start_age + 3),
  list(name = "Retirement tax rate (+/-30%)",    key = "retirement_tax_rate", kind = "pct",    base = retirement_tax_rate, lo = 0.70 * retirement_tax_rate, hi = 1.30 * retirement_tax_rate)
)

tornado_calib <- run_tornado(params_calib)

cat("\n=== Tornado 2: Calibrated realistic uncertainty bands ===\n")
cat(sprintf("Baseline P(ruin) = %.3f%%\n\n", baseline$p_ruin * 100))
print_df_c <- tornado_calib %>%
  arrange(desc(ruin_swing)) %>%
  transmute(
    Parameter          = as.character(parameter),
    `Base | Lo / Hi`   = value_ctx,
    `P(ruin) lo`       = sprintf("%.3f%% (%+.3fpp)", lo_abs, lo_delta),
    `P(ruin) hi`       = sprintf("%.3f%% (%+.3fpp)", hi_abs, hi_delta),
    `Swing (pp)`       = sprintf("%.3f", ruin_swing * 100)
  )
print(print_df_c, row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r tornado_plot_calib, fig.width=11, fig.height=6.5}
ggplot(tornado_calib, aes(y = parameter)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = lo_abs, xend = hi_abs, yend = parameter),
               linewidth = 8, color = "firebrick", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = baseline$p_ruin * 100, linetype = "solid",
             color = "grey20", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(x = lo_abs,
                label = sprintf("%.2f%%\n(%+.2fpp)", lo_abs, lo_delta)),
            hjust = 1.1, size = 2.8, color = "grey15", lineheight = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(x = hi_abs,
                label = sprintf("%.2f%%\n(%+.2fpp)", hi_abs, hi_delta)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 2.8, color = "grey15", lineheight = 0.9) +
  annotate("text", x = baseline$p_ruin * 100,
           y = length(levels(tornado_calib$parameter)) + 0.3,
           label = sprintf("baseline = %.2f%%", baseline$p_ruin * 100),
           size = 3.3, color = "grey20", hjust = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Tornado 2: \u0394 P(ruin) under calibrated realistic uncertainty bands",
       subtitle = "Asymmetric bands reflect what each parameter is actually known to. Compare ordering to Tornado 1.",
       x = "P(ruin) (%)  (absolute on axis, delta in parentheses)", y = "",
       caption = "Reveals which assumptions dominate real-world fragility once uncertainty is honest.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        plot.margin = margin(12, 40, 12, 40))
```

### Bequest-probability impact

P(bequest >= target) responds to the same perturbations, often in the opposite direction. A parameter that makes ruin more likely (e.g., higher spending or higher tax rate) also makes the bequest less likely, but magnitudes differ because ruin is a left-tail event and bequest is a right-tail event.

```{r tornado_bequest_table}
bequest_summary <- tornado_calib %>%
  mutate(
    beq_lo_delta = (bequest_lo - baseline$p_bequest) * 100,
    beq_hi_delta = (bequest_hi - baseline$p_bequest) * 100
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(beq_hi_delta - beq_lo_delta))) %>%
  transmute(
    Parameter           = as.character(parameter),
    `P(ruin) lo`        = sprintf("%+.2fpp", lo_delta),
    `P(ruin) hi`        = sprintf("%+.2fpp", hi_delta),
    `P(beq) lo`         = sprintf("%+.2fpp", beq_lo_delta),
    `P(beq) hi`         = sprintf("%+.2fpp", beq_hi_delta)
  )

print(bequest_summary, row.names = FALSE)
```

### Interpreting the charts

The symmetric tornado measures elasticity, the strategy's per-unit sensitivity to each parameter. The calibrated tornado measures realized fragility, the band of P(ruin) outcomes you should actually expect given honest uncertainty about each input. If the two tornadoes look very different in ordering, it tells you which parameters are not worth worrying to much over (elastic but well-known, so not actually a major real-world concern) and which are (even slight elasticity combined with large real uncertainty produces real swings in outcomes).

The retirement tax rate is typically a silent killer: a 10% elasticity is unremarkable, but 30% realistic uncertainty compounds into a meaningful real-world band, and future rates are genuinely unknowable (legislative risk on brackets, potential state moves, filing-status changes post-spouse's death). Annual spending is typically dominant in both tornadoes because retirees both underestimate actual spending and have genuine uncertainty about future needs (healthcare especially). Current savings is usually the opposite: it moves the bar in the symmetric case, but collapses to nothing in the calibrated case because you actually know your balance.

Social Security enters the calibrated tornado with an **asymmetric** band: -25% to +5%. The SSA trustee reports routinely flag that without legislative action the trust fund shortfall could reduce benefits by 20-25% starting in the mid-2030s, while upside is capped by the statutory benefit formula. The start-age band (+/-5 years) captures the full range from claiming at 62 to delaying to 70, since delayed credits permanently increase the benefit.

# Portfolio Statistics by 5-Year Window

The glide path changes the asset mix every year, so headline lifetime statistics obscure the fact that the retiree is effectively holding a different portfolio at 30 than at 50 or 80. Breaking the lifespan into 5-year windows reveals how the risk-return profile evolves as the Bernstein curves re-weight the portfolio. This section reports: ann. geometric return (CAGR), ann. volatility, Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, maximum drawdown, skewness, 5%VaR, and 5%CVaR.

These are computed per Monte Carlo path within each window and then summarized across paths as the median and the 5th/95th percentiles. Computing per-path and then summarizing is deliberate: averaging returns across paths first and then computing statistics would hide the path-dependence that matters most for a retiree (the sequence-of-returns risk). The median is the "typical" experience; the 5th/95th percentiles bracket what a retiree could plausibly see. The portfolio returns used here are **real** (nominal draws from `ret_array` deflated by realized CIR inflation), so the risk-free rate for Sharpe is set to zero: the natural numeraire is real cash, which has a real arithmetic return of roughly 0.8%, close enough to zero that the distinction is cosmetic at this level of aggregation.

```{r port_stats_windows, fig.width=10, fig.height=6}
# Build per-year portfolio returns for all MC paths under the optimized weights
W_opt <- result$best_weights  # (n_years_total x N_ASSETS), rows sum to 1
# port_returns: (N_MC x n_years_total), real portfolio return each year for each path
# Nominal return deflated by realized CIR inflation to stay consistent with real-terms framing.
port_returns <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = N_MC, ncol = n_years_total)
for (t in seq_len(n_years_total)){
  port_r_nom <- as.numeric(ret_array[, t, ] %*% W_opt[t, ])
  port_returns[, t] <- (1 + port_r_nom) / (1 + infl_array[, t]) - 1
}
# Define 5-year windows spanning the lifespan
window_size <- 5
window_starts <- seq(1, n_years_total, by = window_size)
# Drop the last window if it's a partial (keep only full 5-year blocks)
window_starts <- window_starts[window_starts + window_size - 1 <= n_years_total]
# Per-path window statistics
# Returns a data frame: one row per (window, path) with all the metrics
compute_window_stats <- function(returns_block){
  # returns_block: N_MC x window_size matrix of annual returns
  # Returns per-path stats; caller summarizes across paths
  n_yrs <- ncol(returns_block)
  # Cumulative wealth path within window (starting at 1.0)
  wealth_path <- cbind(1, t(apply(1 + returns_block, 1, cumprod)))
  # Annualized geometric return (CAGR)
  terminal   <- wealth_path[, ncol(wealth_path)]
  cagr       <- terminal^(1 / n_yrs) - 1
  # Annualized volatility (sample SD of annual returns within the window)
  ann_vol    <- apply(returns_block, 1, sd)
  # Sharpe (rf = 0 in real terms)
  sharpe     <- ifelse(ann_vol > 0, cagr / ann_vol, NA_real_)
  # Downside deviation (semi-deviation below 0 target) and Sortino
  downside   <- apply(returns_block, 1, function(r){
    neg <- pmin(r, 0); sqrt(mean(neg^2))
  })
  sortino    <- ifelse(downside > 0, cagr / downside, NA_real_)
  # Max drawdown within window
  max_dd     <- apply(wealth_path, 1, function(w){
    peak <- cummax(w); min(w / peak - 1)
  })
  # Skewness of annual returns (Pearson's moment coefficient)
  skew       <- apply(returns_block, 1, function(r){
    m <- mean(r); s <- sd(r)
    if (s == 0) NA_real_ else mean(((r - m) / s)^3)
  })
  # 5%VaR and CVaR on the annual-return distribution within the window
  # (per-path; uses the n_yrs annual returns for that path)
  var5       <- apply(returns_block, 1, quantile, probs = 0.05, names = FALSE)
  cvar5      <- apply(returns_block, 1, function(r){
    thr <- quantile(r, 0.05, names = FALSE)
    tail_r <- r[r <= thr]
    if (length(tail_r) == 0) thr else mean(tail_r)
  })
  data.frame(cagr, ann_vol, sharpe, sortino, max_dd, skew, var5, cvar5)
}
# Summarize each window across paths (median, p5, p95) + average weights
summarize_window <- function(stats_df, age_start, age_end, weights_block){
  # weights_block: window_size x N_ASSETS slice of W_opt
  avg_w <- colMeans(weights_block)
  names(avg_w) <- asset_names
  
  qfun <- function(x) quantile(x, probs = c(0.05, 0.50, 0.95),
                                names = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE)

  out <- data.frame(
    age_start = age_start,
    age_end   = age_end,
    label     = sprintf("%d-%d", age_start, age_end),
    cagr_p50  = qfun(stats_df$cagr)[2],
    cagr_p05  = qfun(stats_df$cagr)[1],
    cagr_p95  = qfun(stats_df$cagr)[3],
    vol_p50   = qfun(stats_df$ann_vol)[2],
    sharpe_p50  = qfun(stats_df$sharpe)[2],
    sortino_p50 = qfun(stats_df$sortino)[2],
    maxdd_p50 = qfun(stats_df$max_dd)[2],
    maxdd_p05 = qfun(stats_df$max_dd)[1],  # worst 5% of paths
    skew_p50  = qfun(stats_df$skew)[2],
    var5_p50  = qfun(stats_df$var5)[2],
    cvar5_p50 = qfun(stats_df$cvar5)[2]
  )
  for (k in seq_along(avg_w)){
    out[[paste0("w_", names(avg_w)[k])]] <- avg_w[k]
  }
  out
}

window_stats <- do.call(rbind, lapply(window_starts, function(ws){
  we <- ws + window_size - 1
  age_start <- current_age + ws
  age_end   <- current_age + we
  stats_df  <- compute_window_stats(port_returns[, ws:we, drop = FALSE])
  summarize_window(stats_df, age_start, age_end,
                   W_opt[ws:we, , drop = FALSE])
}))
# Printable summary table
summary_tbl <- window_stats %>%
  transmute(
    Window        = label,
    `CAGR (p50)`  = sprintf("%.2f%%", cagr_p50 * 100),
    `CAGR p05/p95`= sprintf("%.2f / %.2f%%", cagr_p05 * 100, cagr_p95 * 100),
    `Vol (p50)`   = sprintf("%.2f%%", vol_p50 * 100),
    `Sharpe`      = sprintf("%.2f", sharpe_p50),
    `Sortino`     = sprintf("%.2f", sortino_p50),
    `MaxDD p50`   = sprintf("%.1f%%", maxdd_p50 * 100),
    `MaxDD p05`   = sprintf("%.1f%%", maxdd_p05 * 100),
    `Skew`        = sprintf("%+.2f", skew_p50),
    `VaR 5%`      = sprintf("%.2f%%", var5_p50 * 100),
    `CVaR 5%`     = sprintf("%.2f%%", cvar5_p50 * 100)
  )

print(summary_tbl, row.names = FALSE)
```

### Return and drawdown across windows

The plot below visualizes the return and drawdown profile alongside the asset mix. The top panel shows median CAGR with the 5th-to-95th percentile band; the middle panel shows median and 5th-percentile (worst-5%-of-paths) max drawdown within each window; the bottom panel shows the average asset weights in each window as a stacked area, so the reader can see which allocation produced which risk-return profile.

```{r port_stats_plot, fig.width=10, fig.height=9}
# Long-format for the weight stack
wt_long <- window_stats %>%
  dplyr::select(label, starts_with("w_")) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "asset", values_to = "weight") %>%
  mutate(asset = factor(sub("^w_", "", asset), levels = asset_names))

p1 <- ggplot(window_stats, aes(x = label, group = 1)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = cagr_p05 * 100, ymax = cagr_p95 * 100),
              fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_line(aes(y = cagr_p50 * 100), color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = cagr_p50 * 100), color = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  labs(title = "Annualized Return (CAGR) by 5-Year Window",
       subtitle = "Line = median across MC paths; band = 5th-95th percentile",
       x = NULL, y = "CAGR (%, real)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p2 <- ggplot(window_stats, aes(x = label, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = maxdd_p50 * 100, color = "Median (p50)"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = maxdd_p05 * 100, color = "Worst 5% (p05)"), linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(aes(y = maxdd_p50 * 100), color = "firebrick", size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Median (p50)" = "firebrick",
                                 "Worst 5% (p05)" = "grey30")) +
  labs(title = "Max Drawdown Within Window",
       x = NULL, y = "Max drawdown (%)", color = "") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")


# show the three plots
print(p1); print(p2)
```

# Conclusion

Across the optimized glide path, the domestic-equity curve traces out a clear V-shape over the lifespan. Equity allocation is high during the accumulation years, declines into a trough around the retirement transition, and then rises again through late retirement. This is precisely the V-shaped equity pattern that Pfau and Kitces (2014) argued should reduce both the probability and severity of portfolio failure. Their argument was based on sequence-of-returns risk: the retirement portfolio is most vulnerable in the early retirement years when withdrawals meet a possibly depleted balance, and easing into equities as retirement progresses lets the portfolio benefit from any long-horizon equity premium while the withdrawal window shortens. The CMA-ES optimizer here, given no shape constraint and no knowledge of their paper, independently converges on the same qualitative shape by simply minimizing P(ruin) over a Monte Carlo of plausible market and mortality paths. The empirical agreement between their 121-path grid search and this continuous-space numerical optimization is notable: two very different methodologies, operating on different underlying return calibrations, arrive at the same non-monotone glide-path prescription. The finding supports their hypothesis that the conventional monotone-decline glide path is not optimal under the joint pressures of sequence risk and a bequest motive, and that rising late-life equity exposure is the appropriate response.

The caveat that applies to both works also applies here: the result is conditional on the assumed mortality, spending, savings, tax, and return distributions. The tornado analysis quantifies that fragility, and the distribution plots show where a scalar P(ruin) or P(bequest) oversimplifies the actual retiree experience. But under any reasonable version of those assumptions, monotone-decline glide paths appear to leave something on the table relative to rising-equity alternatives


# References

Blanchett, D. M. (2007). Dynamic Allocation Strategies for Distribution Portfolios: Determining the Optimal Distribution Glide Path. *Journal of Financial Planning*, 20(12), 68–81.

Hansen, N. (2016). The CMA Evolution Strategy: A Tutorial. *arXiv:1604.00772*.

Lorentz, G.G. (1953). *Bernstein Polynomials.* University of Toronto Press.

McKay, M. D., Beckman, R. J., & Conover, W. J. (1979). A Comparison of Three Methods for Selecting Values of Input Variables in the Analysis of Output from a Computer Code. *Technometrics*, 21(2), 239-245.

Pfau, W. D., & Kitces, M. E. (2014). Reducing Retirement Risk with a Rising Equity Glide Path. *Journal of Financial Planning*, 27(1), 38–45.
